1: A type: the queen of Sheba comes from the ends of the earth to test (lᵉnassōt, H5254 — to test, prove) Solomon's wisdom gained in connection with the name of YHWH. Jesus cites this episode as a direct typological precedent in Matthew 12:42 / Luke 11:31: "The queen of the South will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here." The Solomonic wisdom episode establishes the type — a Gentile ruler drawn irresistibly to divine wisdom — which Christ then exceeds in person.
2: A shadow: the queen's journey to Jerusalem with camels bearing spices, gold, and precious stones anticipates the pilgrimage of the nations to Zion described in Isaiah 60:3-6 ("nations shall come to your light, kings to the brightness of your rising... they shall bring gold and frankincense"). The wealth and the questions she brings represent the Gentile world's deepest longings — wisdom and resources — placed before the one whom God has blessed. The pattern reaches its fulfillment in the Magi of Matt 2 and ultimately in Revelation 21:24-26 (the nations bringing their glory into the New Jerusalem).
3: A shadow: Solomon answers all her questions — "there was nothing hidden from the king that he could not tell her." The completeness of Solomonic wisdom points forward to Christ in whom "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Colossians 2:3). Where Solomon's wisdom is derived (God-given, 1 Kings 3:12) and therefore exhaustible, Christ's wisdom is constitutive: he is the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24). The contrast Solomon/Christ is the NT's explicit theology in Matthew 12:42.
4: A shadow: the queen witnesses all Solomon's wisdom "and the palace he had built." The pairing of wisdom and the built temple-palace complex echoes Proverbs 9:1 ("Wisdom has built her house"). Solomon's establishment of both the house of YHWH and his own palace is the OT's closest picture of the divine wisdom made architecturally present; it shadows the one of whom John says "the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen) among us" — God building his own dwelling in human form (John 1:14).
5: A shadow: the queen is left breathless (wᵉlōʾ-hāyāh bāh rûaḥ, "there was no spirit left in her") by the sight of Solomon's glory. The encounter with divine wisdom and glory is overwhelming to the human visitor. The same response is promised to those who see the glory of Christ: "when he appears, we shall be like him" (1 John 3:2), yet in the meantime the encounter with divine holiness is humbling (Isaiah 6:5; Revelation 1:17). The queen's speechlessness before Solomon's lesser glory is a shadow of the creature's response to the full manifestation of God in Christ.
6: A theme: "The report I heard in my own country about your deeds and your wisdom was true." The theme of a reliable report about a distant king whose glory exceeds all rumor recurs in the NT's theology of the gospel as proclamation: "How will they hear without someone preaching?" (Romans 10:14-15). The queen's acknowledgment that the report was true — even underestimated — anticipates the dynamic where the witness of the church carries an accurate but incomplete account of Christ's glory until he is seen face to face.
7: A theme: "I did not believe the reports until I came and saw with my own eyes. And even now the half was not told me." The gap between report and presence, between second-hand testimony and first-hand encounter, is a recurring NT theme: "Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed" (John 20:29). The queen had only report; she came and saw and was overwhelmed. The eschatological promise is that what believers now receive as testimony ("we have seen and bear witness," 1 John 1:2) will be exceeded by the direct sight promised at Christ's return.
8: A theme: "How fortunate are your men! How fortunate are these servants who stand before you continually and hear your wisdom!" The blessedness of those in continual proximity to divine wisdom is the beatitude of the Solomonic court. Jesus applies a parallel beatitude to his own disciples in Matthew 13:16-17: "Blessed are your eyes, for they see, and your ears, for they hear. Truly I say to you, many prophets and righteous people longed to see what you see, and did not see it." The queen pronounces Solomonic beatitude from the outside; Jesus pronounces Christological beatitude on those who stand before him.
9: A revelation of God: a Gentile queen praises YHWH — "Praise the LORD your God, who delighted in you and set you on the throne of Israel!" Her theological interpretation of Solomon's elevation is correct: YHWH's love for Israel is the reason he raised up this king. The NT carries this structure forward: the exaltation of Jesus to God's right hand is YHWH's demonstration of love for humanity (Acts 2:36; Philippians 2:9-11). Even a pagan witness, when confronted with genuine Solomonic glory, is moved to attribute it to Israel's God — a pattern fulfilled when "every tongue confesses that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father" (Philippians 2:11).
10: A shadow: the queen gives Solomon 120 talents of gold and abundant spices — the gifts of a Gentile ruler to Israel's anointed king. This establishes the OT pattern of Gentile tribute to the Davidic throne that Isaiah 60 develops into an eschatological vision of all nations bringing their wealth to Zion. The Magi's gold, frankincense, and myrrh (Matthew 2:11) are the NT's first fulfillment of this pattern; Revelation 21:24-26 describes its final form. The queen's gift begins a typological sequence that ends at the throne of the Lamb.
11: A shadow: the almugwood brought from Ophir along with gold and precious stones — all for the temple and its music. The beauty and richness of material creation consecrated for worship is a consistent OT shadow of the eschatological reality in which "the nations will bring their glory and honor" into the new creation (Revelation 21:26). No such almugwood has been seen since: the uniqueness of the Solomonic era is the OT's closest approximation of the world made fully right, now forever past.
12: A shadow: the almugwood made into supports (misʿādôt, railings or steps) for the temple and into instruments of praise — lyres and harps for the musicians. Creation's material beauty (wood from distant lands) is transfigured into instruments of worship. This foreshadows the new creation in which all things find their ultimate purpose in the praise of God: "And every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, and all that is in them, saying, 'To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever'" (Revelation 5:13).
13: A shadow: Solomon gives the queen "everything she desired and asked for, in addition to all that he gave her out of his royal generosity" (kᵉyaḏ hammelek šᵉlōmōh, "according to the hand of King Solomon"). The king's generosity exceeds the visitor's request — this is the OT pattern of divine abundance. Jesus makes this the pattern of the Father's giving: "Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find" (Matthew 7:7); and Paul extends it: "He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for all of us, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?" (Romans 8:32). Solomon's open-handedness shadows the generosity of the one greater than Solomon.
14: A shadow: 666 talents of gold arriving to Solomon in a single year — the numeric peak of Solomonic wealth. The number represents the apex of earthly royal glory, and its very splendor conceals its fragility: this is the moment before the fall narrated in 1 Kings 11. The weight of gold is not given to identify Solomon with Revelation's beast (the NT contextualizes 666 differently, Revelation 13:18); it marks rather the outer limit of what the greatest human king could accumulate. What Solomon could collect in weight of gold, Christ offers in imperishable currency: "the riches of his grace" (Ephesians 1:7) that no accounting can exhaust.
15: A shadow: the enumeration of Solomon's income from merchants, traders, kings of Arabia, and governors of the land. The breadth of the tribute network foreshadows the universal tribute described in Psalms 72:10-11: "May the kings of Tarshish and of the coastlands render him tribute; may the kings of Sheba and Seba bring gifts! May all kings fall down before him, all nations serve him!" — a psalm read messianically in the NT tradition. Solomon's tributary network is the historical shadow of the eschatological reality.
16: A shadow: the two hundred large shields of hammered gold placed in the House of the Forest of Lebanon. The royal armory of gold — weapons that could never actually be used in battle (they are display objects) — represents the ceremonial magnificence of the Solomonic kingdom. The contrast with Christ's kingship is illuminating: his instruments of conquest are not gold shields but weakness and the cross (1 Corinthians 1:25), weapons that actually win the decisive battle against sin and death (Colossians 2:15).
17: A shadow: three hundred smaller shields of hammered gold, also placed in the House of Lebanon. The duplication of royal golden shields underscores the ceremonial nature of Solomonic glory — magnificence that is primarily visual. The NT counterpart to royal display is not ornamentation but mission: Christ equips his people with the "full armor of God" (Ephesians 6:10-17), which is functional rather than decorative, designed for actual combat against spiritual powers.
18: A shadow: the great ivory throne overlaid with the finest gold. The throne of the Davidic king is the OT's earthly instantiation of the divine throne — a human approximation of the heavenly reality described in Isaiah 6, Ezekiel 1, and Daniel 7. The Solomonic throne's magnificence (ivory + gold, lions, six steps) shadows the throne described in Rev 4-5 and the promise to believers to share Christ's throne: "The one who conquers, I will grant him to sit with me on my throne, as I also conquered and sat down with my Father on his throne" (Revelation 3:21).
19: A shadow: the six-stepped throne with two lions beside the armrests — the royal lion flanking the king. The lion as royal emblem connects to the tribal blessing of Judah ("a lion's cub," Genesis 49:9) and to Christ as "the Lion of the tribe of Judah" (Revelation 5:5). Solomon's lion-flanked throne is the Davidic-dynasty shadow of the Lamb who is also the Lion — the enthroned ruler before whom every knee will bow (Philippians 2:10).
20: A shadow: twelve lions standing on the six steps, six on each side — "nothing like it had been made in any kingdom." The twelve lions may echo the twelve tribes of Israel, surrounding the king who rules over all Israel. The uniqueness of the Solomonic throne ("nothing like it") prefigures the absolute uniqueness of Christ's throne — the only throne before which all creation will one day stand (Revelation 20:11-12).
21: A shadow: all Solomon's drinking cups are gold; all vessels in the House of the Forest of Lebanon are pure gold. Silver is not considered worth anything in Solomon's day. The currency devaluation caused by Solomon's wealth is a shadow of the eschatological reversal where the former costly things of earth become commonplace in the new creation (Revelation 21:21, "the street of the city was pure gold"). What Solomon achieves economically for a generation, the new Jerusalem establishes permanently for eternity.
22: A shadow: the fleet of Tarshish ships returning every three years with gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks — the exotic wealth of the whole world flowing to Solomon's court. Psalms 72:10 ("May the kings of Tarshish and of the coastlands render him tribute") is the messianic psalm that applies this Tarshish-tribute pattern to the ideal Davidic king. The Solomonic reality partially fulfills Ps 72 during his reign; the full fulfillment awaits the reign of David's greater son.
23: A type: "King Solomon surpassed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom." This is the high-water mark of Davidic-line glory, and Jesus explicitly identifies himself as what Solomon points to: "something greater than Solomon is here" (Matthew 12:42). The superlative language (hāyāh gedôl) is picked up and exceeded in the NT's christological superlatives — "the name above every name" (Philippians 2:9), "far above all rule and authority" (Ephesians 1:21), "the firstborn over all creation" (Colossians 1:15). Solomon's greatness among kings is a type of Christ's greatness over all things.
24: A type: "All the peoples of the earth sought an audience with Solomon to hear the wisdom that God had placed in his heart." The gathering of all peoples before the wise king is the OT prototype of the eschatological gathering before Christ. The wisdom "placed in his heart" by God (H3820 + H430) is a human impartation, temporary and derived; Colossians 2:3 identifies Christ as the one in whom "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" — not placed in him but inherent in him as the eternal Word (John 1:1-3). What Solomon offers by divine grant, Christ is by nature.
25: A shadow: year after year every visitor brings gifts — silver, gold, garments, weapons, spices, horses, mules. The annual pilgrimage tribute to Solomon anticipates the eschatological pilgrimage of the nations to the Davidic Messiah. Psalms 72:15 ("May prayer be made for him continually, and blessings invoked for him all the day! May there be abundance of grain in the land... and people be blessed in him, all nations call him blessed!") reads the Solomonic pattern as a messianic portrait. The annual tribute shadow reaches its fulfillment in Revelation 21:24-26.
26: A shadow: Solomon accumulates 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horsemen. The Torah's law for kings (Deuteronomy 17:16) explicitly forbids the king to "multiply horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt in order to acquire many horses." Solomon violates this command directly (v28 makes the Egypt connection explicit). The accumulated chariots and horsemen are the beginning of the Torah-violation pattern that culminates in v1-8 of 1 Kings 11. The ideal king of Israel is not to trust in horses (Psalms 33:17; Isaiah 31:1); the shadow of Christ's kingship is power made perfect in weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9), not military accumulation.
27: A shadow: silver made as common as stones in Jerusalem, cedars as plentiful as sycamore figs. The transformation of precious materials into commonplace abundance is the OT picture of eschatological prosperity — Isaiah 60:17 ("Instead of bronze I will bring gold, and instead of iron I will bring silver"). Solomon's economic transformation of Jerusalem for one generation shadows the permanent transformation of the new creation. But what Solomon achieves by accumulation, the new creation achieves by the gift of God's presence (Revelation 21:23, "the city has no need of sun... for the glory of God gives it light").
28: A shadow: Solomon's horses imported from Egypt and Kue. The return to Egypt for horses is an explicit violation of Deuteronomy 17:16. The king who should model trust in YHWH over military power (Psalms 20:7, "Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD our God") turns to Egypt instead. This verse marks the narrative's quiet turn: Solomon begins to look less like the ideal covenant king and more like a standard ancient Near Eastern monarch. The contrast with Christ is sharp — Christ refused to call down twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53) and entered Jerusalem on a donkey, not a war horse (Matthew 21:5; Zechariah 9:9).
29: A shadow: the chariot trade routes — Egypt to Hittites and Aram, 600 shekels of silver per chariot, 150 per horse. Solomon as international arms trader is as far from the Deuteronomic ideal as it is possible to be. The king who should trust in YHWH's name (Deuteronomy 17:18-20, reading the Torah daily to humble himself) is instead running a weapons business with Israel's traditional enemies. The corruption of Solomonic kingship, fully revealed in 1 Kings 11, is already audible here. Christ's economy inverts this: he does not trade in weapons of war but gives the "sword of the Spirit" freely (Ephesians 6:17).
